your_wellness_goals.your_initial_health_.answer: Reduce chronic lower back and neck pain from a daily 7–8/10 to a more manageable level so I can get through a full workday at my desk without having to constantly stand up, stretch, or take pain medication.

Improve posture and flexibility so I can comfortably sit, stand, and walk for longer periods, return to light jogging, and be able to play on the floor with my kids without feeling stiff and sore afterward.
Increase overall energy levels and reduce stress and tension so I am not exhausted by mid-afternoon and can be more present and patient with my family in the evenings.

how_are_you_doing.physical_changes.answer:
Over the past three months of care, I’ve noticed several physical changes that feel significant compared to where I started. My lower back pain used to sit at around a 7/10 most days, especially after long stretches at the computer, and now it’s more consistently around a 3–4/10, with some days dropping to a 2/10 when I’ve slept well and done my stretches. The sharp, stabbing pain I used to get when bending forward to tie my shoes or pick up something from the floor has mostly turned into more of a mild tightness. I can now bend down to pick up my 4-year-old daughter without that sudden catch in my back that used to make me freeze in place. 

My neck and upper back feel looser as well. Before starting care, I would get frequent tension headaches, especially at the end of a workday. Those have decreased noticeably; I used to have them 3–4 times per week, and now it’s maybe once every week or two, and they are less intense and respond better to stretching and hydration. I also notice that my shoulders don’t sit up by my ears as much, and I can roll them back more easily without that grinding or pulling sensation.

In terms of mobility, I can twist and rotate more comfortably. For example, when backing out of the driveway, I can turn to look over my shoulder without feeling like my lower back locks up. I’ve also slowly returned to light jogging—right now I’m doing 15–20 minutes of very easy jog/walk intervals twice a week, which I couldn’t do at all before without paying for it with pain the next day. My hamstrings and hips feel less tight; I can now touch mid-shin comfortably, and on good days almost reach my ankles when bending forward, whereas before I could barely get past my knees.

I still have some discomfort when I sit for more than an hour at my desk, especially if I forget to adjust my workstation or take breaks. The pain is not as intense, but there is still a dull ache that reminds me I’m not 100% yet. Overall, though, I feel physically stronger and more stable, especially in my core. The home exercises you gave me—like the bird dogs, bridges, and gentle hip stretches—seem to be helping my back feel more supported and less fragile.

how_are_you_doing.health_changes.answer:
From a general health perspective, I’ve noticed a few positive changes since starting this treatment plan and making some of the lifestyle adjustments you recommended. I used to catch every little cold that went around my office or my kids’ daycare, and while I have still gotten sick once or twice, the symptoms didn’t linger as long and didn’t knock me out the way they used to. I used to have a lingering cough for weeks after a cold; this winter, the congestion and sore throat cleared up in about a week.

I’ve also been more consistent with gentle movement and daily walks, which I think is helping my circulation and overall sense of well-being. My digestion seems to have improved slightly as well. I’m not as bloated in the evenings, and I’ve been more regular, which I suspect is a combination of drinking more water, cutting back on late-night snacking, and moving more throughout the day.

My blood pressure at my last primary care visit was a bit lower than it has been in the past (it was 124/78 instead of in the 130s/80s), and while I know that can fluctuate, I do feel like the combination of reduced pain, better sleep, and more exercise is having a positive effect on my overall health. I’m also relying less on over-the-counter pain medications; I used to take ibuprofen several times a week for back and neck pain, and now I might take it once every week or two, usually only after a particularly long day of yard work or if I’ve overdone it with lifting.

how_are_you_doing.emotional_changes.answer:
Emotionally, I feel more stable and less irritable than I did before starting care. Chronic pain used to make me short-tempered, especially in the evenings when I was tired and my back was aching. I would snap at my kids or be impatient with my wife over small things, and then feel guilty later. As the pain has become more manageable, I notice I have a slightly longer fuse and more emotional bandwidth to handle the normal chaos of family life.

I’ve also been feeling a bit more hopeful overall. When I first came in, I was worried that my back issues were just something I would have to live with indefinitely and that they would continue to get worse. Seeing measurable improvements—even if I’m not all the way where I want to be yet—has made me feel less anxious about the future. I’m more confident that I can keep working in my current job and still be physically capable of doing the things I enjoy, like hiking and playing with my kids.

I still have moments of stress and occasional anxiety, especially around work deadlines and finances, but I don’t feel as overwhelmed as I did a few months ago. Doing the breathing exercises you showed me and trying to take short breaks from the computer during the day has helped me feel more grounded. I’ve also noticed that when I do feel stressed, I’m more aware of how it shows up in my body (tight shoulders, clenched jaw), and I’m better at catching it early and doing a quick stretch or walk instead of just pushing through it.

how_are_you_doing.energy_stress_levels_c.answer:
My energy levels have improved, though I wouldn’t say they are perfect yet. Before starting care, I would hit a wall around 2–3 PM almost every day and feel like I needed a second large coffee just to stay functional. Now, while I still get a bit tired in the afternoon, it’s not as severe, and some days I can push through with just water and a short walk instead of caffeine. I wake up feeling slightly more rested most mornings, which I’m sure is related to the fact that I’m sleeping more deeply and not waking up as often from back pain.

In terms of sleep, I used to wake up 3–4 times a night trying to find a comfortable position. Now I generally wake up once, maybe twice on a bad night, and often I can roll to my side or adjust my pillow and go back to sleep without fully waking up. I started using a small pillow between my knees like you suggested, which has helped a lot with my lower back. I’m averaging about 6.5–7 hours of more solid sleep, compared to 5–6 hours of restless sleep before.

Stress-wise, work is still demanding—I manage a small software development team and there are always deadlines—but I feel better equipped to handle it. I’ve set reminders to stand up and stretch every hour, and those short breaks seem to reset my brain a bit. I’ve also cut back on late-night screen time, which I think has helped my nervous system wind down more easily. Overall, I’d say my stress is still present but less overwhelming, and my energy is more steady throughout the day instead of being a roller coaster.

how_are_you_doing.new_health_challenges_.answer:
The main new stressor over the last month has been that my mother, who lives about 40 minutes away, had a minor fall and sprained her wrist. She’s mostly okay, but I’ve been driving over there once or twice a week to help her with groceries, trash, and some light housework until she’s fully back to normal. This has added some extra time pressure to my schedule, especially on weeknights, and there have been a couple of days where I skipped my usual stretching routine because I was getting home late.

Work has also been a bit more intense recently because we’re rolling out a new software release, and I’ve been putting in some extra hours to coordinate with my team and fix last-minute issues. I’ve tried to be mindful about my posture and take breaks, but there have definitely been a few late nights where I noticed my back tightening up again.

On the health side, I did have one minor flare-up of my lower back pain about three weeks ago after I spent most of a Saturday raking leaves and lifting heavy bags in the yard. The pain spiked back up to about a 6/10 that evening and the next day, and I was worried I had undone all my progress. However, with ice, some gentle stretching, and sticking to the exercises you gave me, it settled back down within a few days. That episode reminded me that I still need to pace myself and not jump too quickly into heavier physical tasks, even when I’m feeling better.

Otherwise, I haven’t had any new diagnoses or major health issues. My main challenges right now are balancing work, family responsibilities, and caring for my mom, while still making time for the self-care routines (stretches, walks, better sleep habits) that are clearly helping my back and overall well-being.
